Standardization was noted to be less effective than other methods particularly concerning accessory canal clearance."}, {'title': 'Root Canals: A Comprehensive Guide', 'author': 'Dr. Jane Smith', 'chapter': 'Techniques for Effective Root Canal Treatment', 'page': 56, 'content': "While straightforward, the traditional approach has limitations especially related to incomplete preparation of irregularities inside the canal walls leading to residual infection risks post-treatment"}, {'doi_link': 'https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/full/10.1177/0099299X19837465'}]

Based on the retrieved evidence, several studies highlight specific drawbacks associated with the standard technique, including limited ability to clear out accessory canals effectively and an increased likelihood of leaving some parts of the root canal untreated. This aligns strongly with option C stating that accessory canals are not removed efficiently.
Option B suggests reliance on natural file shape rather than active manipulation, which might indeed play a role but does not address the key issue identified here—namely, the inefficiency in removing all types of debris and structures within the canal.
Therefore, I concur with Option D being correct since it encompasses both points mentioned earlier plus adds another valid concern about accessory canals.
